hard.next_step: 6.29.26 KK - shared ROI document based on public data of SABIC shared Nippon Gas presentation from Horizon Delta Case study bc SAP replacement Oil and Gas Zycus Overview on challenges and how zycus solves for those Waiting on SAP integration video - will share tomorrow 6.11.26 KK - tech convo went well, looking to do live SAP demo integration waiting on availabilty from SABIC, shared budgetary pricing based on experience. We will have to update based on their inputs 6.8.26 KK - technical conversation on Wednesday 5.27.26 KK - demo last week went well. FU today on how their internal conversations were going and next steps 5.20.26 KK - discovery last week, demo tomorrow 5.6.26 KK - requesting Presales, looking to do discovery next week and demo following week.
hard.ais_why:
• The Merlin product line is empty and Products does not include Merlin, Agentic AI or ANA - only iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement and eInvoicing. The description states bluntly: "informal process - no RFP - demos and decision. No strong compelling event". The buyer has not made AI a selection criterion.
• The May 6 2026 discovery call (31 minutes) with Klimack, Harris and Pacheco captured pain points centered on convoluted SAP processes, SAP SRM end-of-life, ease of use, EDI catalog integration and three-way invoice match. Must-haves: invoicing, e-catalogs and SRM replacement. None of the buyer quotes reference AI, Merlin, agentic or autonomous capability.
• AI importance is rated 'Important', but that is a Zycus-side field with no supporting buyer evidence. The actual product mix sold and the pain points discussed contain zero AI signals. Sourcing and CLM are only "nice-to-have" per the buyer - the narrow P2P scope further dampens any AI surface. This is Resistant.
hard.primary_competitor: Undisclosed formal top-5 P2P shortlist / status-quo (no compelling event)

north_star_verdict.headline: Live, technically-strong Formal Evaluation re-accelerating into two July group demos (procurement 14 Jul, supply-chain 15 Jul), but still single-threaded through a process-level champion (Joseph Pacheco), no economic buyer has ever appeared, and there is no SABIC-specific value case ahead of a cost-ranked top-5-to-top-2 down-select.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 06 May 2026; in Formal Evaluation throughout (~61 days in-stage). Close 01 Nov 2026 = 118 days out. Buyer replied by email 01-02 Jul 2026 and two group demos are booked 14-15 Jul (server pulse: LIVE). Six-plus of the touchpoints deep-read across discovery, tailored demo, SAP technical deep-dive and CLM demo. Remaining gates (down-select, SABIC-data ROI, EB sign-off, InfoSec, legal, live SAP integration test) are sequenceable inside 118 days ONLY if the EB gate opens within 30 days; engagement is real but the gates that decide it (power + value case) are untouched, so Best Case is not defensible.
evidence: Buyer re-engaged 30 Jun-02 Jul 2026 requesting a bigger-group procurement demo and confirming July availability; two demos booked 14-15 Jul 2026 (server pulse: LIVE) - genuine two-way pull, not rep push.

Joseph Pacheco (Avoma discovery 06 May 2026): the decision is 'a combined effort ... people at my same role in Europe ... will be involved with looking at and helping to select' - an approval committee above Joseph that Zycus has never met.
Ivana Harris (Task 19 Jun 2026): 'I have reviewed and now waiting on leadership discussions regarding evaluations' - the decision has moved to an unnamed leadership layer with no Zycus contact.

Cost is an explicit ranking criterion (Joseph Pacheco, 06 May: 'rank them ... top 5 ... talk to the top two') yet Zycus has shared only a public-data ROI and a Nippon Gas case study (29 Jun 2026) - no SABIC-specific business case exists for the buyer to rank on.
AIS 3.0 / Negative and SF Description note an informal process with 'no RFP ... no strong compelling event' beyond the SAP SRM end-of-life - status-quo inertia is a live threat, not just a rival.

metrics.narrative: Pain is well articulated but never quantified with SABIC's own numbers. On the 06 May 2026 discovery Joseph Pacheco named must-haves centered on replacing end-of-life SAP SRM, high-volume invoice three-way matching ('we don't wanna go to a manual invoicing process'), e-catalog/EDI buying and spend visibility, while Klimack stressed end-user ease of use over a 'convoluted' SAP structure. Karson Keogh shared a public-data ROI and a Nippon Gas oil-and-gas transformation case study on 29 Jun 2026, but leadership explicitly asked (Ivana Harris, 26 Jun) for a case study tied to the ROI, signalling the generic model has not landed. With cost an explicit ranking criterion, the move is to collect SABIC's real invoice volumes and cycle times and build a SABIC-data ROI before the top-5-to-top-2 down-select.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has appeared on any touchpoint. Joseph Pacheco drives the evaluation but on 06 May 2026 described 'a combined effort... people at my same role in Europe' - a US/Europe committee above him Zycus has not met - and on 19 Jun 2026 Ivana Harris confirmed the decision sits with 'leadership discussions regarding evaluations' with no name attached. Budgetary pricing ($520K ARR software + $620K one-time services) was shared 12 Jun and a public-data ROI on 29 Jun, yet there is no VP-Procurement meeting and no written approval path. For a $518k P2P carve-out this is the single largest risk and the reason Best Case is indefensible. The move is to have Joseph Pacheco name and broker the introduction to the Americas budget owner, using the champion who authored the process as the path to power rather than cold-outreach.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are emerging but not formally documented. Joseph Pacheco (06 May 2026) set the explicit ranking axes: 'cost information, implementation timeline ... rank them ... top 5 ... top two.' The evaluation then tested SAP integration depth (10 Jun technical deep-dive, where Jacco and IT probed batch vs trigger-based sync and certified adapters) and CLM/legal fit (17 Jun, where Brian said 'for me it looks great' and legal probed vendor-paper vs own-paper handling, external-counsel provisioning and renewal tracking). Ease of use for end users over 'convoluted' SAP and single-platform sourcing-to-invoicing integration are recurring must-haves. What is not confirmed is a written scoring rubric or weighting, so the move is to co-author the evaluation criteria list with Joseph before the down-select.
sources: Avoma CLM demo 17 Jun 2026 (Brian, Anna, Jeroen Verhulst)

decision_process.narrative: Read from the call sequence the buyer is running a broadening functional evaluation: discovery (06, 15 May) -> tailored demo (21 May) -> SAP integration/InfoSec technical deep-dive (10 Jun) -> CLM/legal demo (17 Jun) -> and now module-by-module group demos booked for procurement (14 Jul) and supply-chain (15 Jul). The audience has widened from procurement to IT/ERP to legal to a 'bigger group of Procurement experts from operational perspective' (Ivana Harris, 30 Jun). Joseph Pacheco (06 May) defined a top-5 ranking then top-2 deep review before a combined US/Europe decision. What is missing is the approval chain above Joseph and any dated vendor-selection deadline; on 17 Jun Brian noted he would speak with his manager Kathleen on her return. The move is a written mutual evaluation timeline with a named down-select date.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are partially visible from the 17 Jun CLM demo: SABIC confirmed DocuSign for eSignatures, a homegrown/SharePoint contract repository, multi-level signature authority that varies by contract type ('it could be multiple level depending on ... the signature authority of different people', Joseph Pacheco), and an outsourced external legal-review step in Cartagena (Anna). Contact roles name the legal owners - Brittany Somerville (Sr. Business Counsel) and Brandon Lacey (Senior Paralegal) - and Brian referenced escalating to his manager Kathleen. The 10 Jun session covered InfoSec posture (ISO 27001, SOC 1 and SOC 2 Type 2). No MSA/SOW, redline owner or procurement channel is confirmed and there is no contract-expiration forcing date. With a Nov close, the move is to name a paper owner and map the legal/procurement signature path now so a commercial 'yes' does not stall in redlining.
sources: Avoma CLM demo 17 Jun 2026 (Joseph Pacheco, Anna, Brian)

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is concrete and owner-attributed. On 06 May 2026 Klimack said SAP SRM is 'more or less end of life ... we're looking for something to replace SRM' and the setup 'can be kinda convoluted ... we're looking to make it as simple, straightforward for the end user.' Joseph Pacheco named high-volume invoice three-way matching, e-catalog buying and scattered multi-ERP, multi-country contracts as must-haves. On the 17 Jun CLM demo the buyer confirmed contracts are 'scattered' across homegrown systems plus SAP attachments with manual renewal tracking - Brian described a real 20-year auto-renewal 'nightmare' from a prior org. The SAP SRM end-of-life is the compelling event, though no hard sunset date was given, which is exactly why do-nothing remains a competing option.
sources: Avoma CLM demo 17 Jun 2026 (Brian, Devika synthesis)

champion.narrative: Joseph Pacheco (procurement, ABS P2P evaluation lead) is the champion-in-development: he convenes procurement, IT and legal, sets the top-5 ranking process, and on the 17 Jun CLM demo actively framed the agenda for legal and kept the group focused. Ivana Harris (RGP Consultant) is the day-to-day coordinator driving all scheduling and document requests. But Joseph's advocacy is still process-level, not sponsorship - he has not introduced Zycus to the budget owner or lobbied for us against the undisclosed rivals, and the decision has already moved to an unnamed 'leadership' layer he has not opened. The single act that converts him from coordinator to champion is brokering the economic-buyer meeting armed with a SABIC-data business case. The move is to test that sponsorship by requesting the introduction.

competition.narrative: A competitive Source-to-Pay displacement, not sole-sourced, and the direct P2P shortlist is still hidden. Incumbents being displaced are SAP SRM (end-of-life, the compelling event) and SAP Ariba (Europe sourcing/onboarding, 'they're gonna stop using it'), plus a third-party sourcing-orchestration pilot whose gap is no invoicing integration - Zycus's single-platform wedge. Joseph Pacheco described a formal top-5 narrowing to top-2 (06 May 2026) but has never disclosed who the other four are, so the live direct rivals are unknown and rank as the highest current competitive risk. The SF Description also flags an informal, no-RFP process with no strong compelling event, meaning status-quo inertia is itself a real alternative. The move is to ask Joseph directly who else is in the top five, then tailor differentiation and pricing before the down-select.

deal_movement.summary: LIVE and re-accelerating. After a mid-June 'waiting on leadership' pause, the buyer pulled the process forward: requested a bigger-group procurement demo (30 Jun), confirmed July availability, and two module demos are booked 14-15 Jul. Amount was raised 250k -> 518k (11 Jun) as full P2P scope firmed; budgetary pricing and implementation cost were shared; a Nippon Gas ROI/case study was delivered 29 Jun via a new Showpad shared space.

items.change: Buyer requested a bigger-group procurement demo 'from operational perspective' and confirmed 14-15 Jul availability; procurement and supply-chain demos booked.
Nippon Gas transformation ROI/case study and SAP-replacement collateral shared via new SABIC x Zycus Showpad shared space (Pacheco and Jeroen Verhulst invited).
Ivana Harris signalled the decision moved to 'leadership discussions regarding evaluations'.
CLM/legal demo held for Brian (Head of Legal US), Brittany Somerville, Brandon Lacey and procurement; buyer reaction positive ('for me it looks great').
Implementation cost shared ($620K one-time services on $520K ARR software) after buyer asked.
Amount raised 250000 -> 518000 as full P2P scope firmed.

competitive_position.summary: Ranked by CURRENT threat: (1) the undisclosed formal top-5 P2P shortlist is the single strongest live threat - Zycus is one of five narrowing to two on a cost-and-timeline ranking (Joseph Pacheco, 06 May 2026) and the rivals are unnamed; (2) status-quo / do-nothing is a genuine alternative given an informal, no-RFP process with 'no strong compelling event' beyond SAP SRM end-of-life; (3) SAP SRM is the incumbent being displaced (end-of-life, low current threat but the compelling event); (4) SAP Ariba in Europe is being dropped (declined); (5) a third-party sourcing-orchestration pilot lacks invoicing integration - Zycus's single-platform wedge. No new named rival surfaced this run, so none is asserted.

competitors.name: Status quo / do-nothing (SAP SRM kept, manual process)
competitors.quote: informal process - no RFP - demos and decision. No strong compelling event.
competitors.how_we_win: Convert the SAP SRM end-of-life into a dated forcing event and quantify the cost of staying manual (invoice matching, missed renewals - Brian's 20-year auto-renewal example) so inaction has a price.

competitors.name: SAP SRM (incumbent being replaced)
competitors.quote: We run SAP SRM here. That's more or less end of life. We're looking for something to replace SRM.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the modern, easy-to-use SRM replacement and anchor the Nov timeline to the SRM sunset.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba (Europe sourcing/onboarding)
competitors.quote: For sourcing in Europe they use Ariba. They're gonna stop using it. We don't use it here in Americas.
competitors.how_we_win: Contrast one integrated S2P platform against fragmented Ariba; cite the painful Ariba vendor re-onboarding to neutralize migration fear in the live SAP demo.

competitors.name: Third-party sourcing-orchestration pilot (vendor unnamed)
competitors.quote: Pilot covers source-to-contract activities such as RFP creation and bid analysis, but it lacks integration into invoicing and order-to-pay.
competitors.how_we_win: Lead with sourcing-to-invoicing integration as the gap the pilot cannot close; position Zycus as the single-platform answer, not a two-tool stack.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong functional fit on the buyer's stated must-haves (SRM replacement, invoice matching, SAP integration, CLM), with the exposure being commercial (cost ranking with no SABIC-specific ROI) and organizational (no EB, undisclosed rivals) rather than product.

items.criterion: Replace end-of-life SAP SRM with an easy-to-use platform
items.quote: That's more or less end of life. We're looking for something to replace SRM ... as simple, straightforward for the end user.

items.criterion: Native, low-effort SAP integration (certified adapters / iSaaS)
items.quote: It definitely gives a better understanding of the type of interfacing and integration.

items.criterion: CLM/contract centralization for scattered contracts + legal
Cost / implementation timeline ranking vs peers
items.quote: cost information, implementation timeline ... rank them.
waiting on leadership discussions regarding evaluations.

items.requirement: Provide a customer case study supporting the Zycus ROI document.
items.quote: Leadership has requested a case study regarding the Zycus ROI document you sent over.
items.source: Task/Email 26 Jun 2026; delivered via Showpad (Nippon Gas) 29 Jun 2026

items.requirement: Run a bigger-group procurement demo from an operational perspective.
items.quote: Does your team have availability to have a demo session for a bigger group of Procurement experts from operational perspective?

items.requirement: Share implementation cost alongside the software proposal.
items.quote: Is there an implementation cost? I did not see it on the proposal. Can you share that as soon as possible?

items.deliverable: Send the recorded live SAP-integration video demonstrating the certified connector.
items.grounding_quote: Ronit and the team recently recorded a short video demonstrating our live int... Waiting on SAP integration video - will share tomorrow.
items.source: Task 02 Jul 2026 + SF Next Step (Karson Keogh)

items.deliverable: Provide named Zycus customer success stories/references to Ivana for the evaluation.
items.grounding_quote: If you're referring to customer success stories from other Zycus clients, we're happy to provide those as well to showcase real-world outcomes.

items.deliverable: Provide SABIC commercial inputs (volumes/scope) so Zycus can build a tailored proposal.
items.grounding_quote: Are you able to provide the commercial inputs needed for us to prepare the proposal? I've attached an Excel file that outlines the information we're looking for.

items.deliverable: Share the outcome of internal leadership evaluation / down-select decision.
items.grounding_quote: I have reviewed and now waiting on leadership discussions regarding evaluations.

items.area: Economic buyer / budget owner never named or met
items.quote: a combined effort ... people at my same role in Europe ... will be involved with looking at and helping to select.
We'll rank top 5 ... talk further with the top two.

items.area: No SABIC-specific quantified business case / ROI
items.quote: Leadership has requested a case study regarding the Zycus ROI document.

items.area: No dated vendor-selection deadline or compelling event
items.quote: informal process - no RFP - demos and decision. No strong compelling event.

best_practice_check.summary: Formal Evaluation, engaged and multi-functional but single-threaded to power; the three highest-impact levers are opening the economic buyer, building a SABIC-data value case, and unmasking the shortlist.
flags: Break single-thread to power: Joseph Pacheco owns the process but the decision has moved to an unnamed US/Europe leadership layer Zycus has never met - broker that introduction before the down-select.
Win the cost-ranked comparison with proof, not a public-data model: build a SABIC-specific ROI from their real invoice volumes and cycle times, since leadership explicitly rejected the generic ROI.
Unmask the top-5 field: ask Joseph who the other finalists are so differentiation and pricing can be tailored, and convert SAP SRM end-of-life into a dated forcing event to beat do-nothing.

items.name: Brittany Somerville
items.title: Sr. Business Counsel
items.risk: Legal reviewer engaged on CLM; weight on final selection unknown.

items.name: Brandon Lacey
items.title: Senior Paralegal
items.risk: Legal support engaged on CLM; sentiment unknown.

items.name: Hassan Sheraz
items.title: Sr. Manager, IT ERP
items.risk: Technical validator for SAP integration; no commercial authority.

items.name: Pamela Summers
items.title: Manager, Direct Material Procurement North America Sites
items.risk: Named procurement stakeholder not yet directly engaged on a read call; a multi-thread target for the 14 Jul group demo.

champion_strength.summary: Joseph Pacheco is a developing champion: he runs the evaluation, convenes procurement/IT/legal and framed the 17 Jun legal agenda, but his advocacy is process-level and he has not brokered the economic-buyer meeting - the ask that will prove whether he is a true champion. Ivana Harris (external RGP consultant) is a supportive coach on logistics, not a power sponsor.
champion_strength.source: Avoma discovery 06 May 2026 + CLM demo 17 Jun 2026 (Joseph Pacheco)

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus is proactively pushing the AI story - Devika demoed the Merlin agentic-AI intake front-door in Teams and near-future AI-assisted redlining on the 17 Jun CLM call - but the buyer never asked for AI and made it no part of the selection. AIS 3.0/Negative reflects a P2P-modernization buy, not an AI buy; do not over-index the Merlin narrative as the wedge here.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS_Score 3.0 / AIS_Status Negative: product mix is core P2P (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing) with no Merlin line, and buyer pain (SRM replacement, invoice matching, ease of use) contains zero AI asks. Zycus surfaced Merlin intake and AI redlining unprompted on 17 Jun; the calls corroborate the field - AI is a nice-to-have, not a criterion.

items.detail: No economic buyer identified or met; decision has moved to an unnamed US/Europe leadership committee above Joseph Pacheco.
items.source: Avoma discovery 06 May 2026 + Task 19 Jun 2026 (Ivana Harris)

items.detail: Cost is an explicit ranking criterion but only a public-data ROI exists; leadership rejected the generic model and asked for a case study - no SABIC-specific value case to justify $518k.
Single-threaded through a process-level champion; the top-5 rival field is undisclosed and an informal no-RFP process leaves status-quo as a live alternative.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is live and re-accelerating - buyer-initiated bigger-group demos booked 14-15 Jul after a brief mid-June leadership pause - so the deal is not cooling; the open questions are power and value case, not engagement.
items: Buyer pulled the next meetings (bigger-group procurement + supply-chain demos, 14-15 Jul 2026).
Positive functional reactions on CLM (Brian, 17 Jun) and integration (Jacco, 10 Jun).
Decision paused at an unnamed leadership layer (Ivana Harris, 19 Jun) - watch, not cold.

scope_change.to: $518k full P2P suite (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing)
scope_change.detail: Amount raised 250000 -> 518000 on 11 Jun 2026 as full Source-to-Pay scope firmed across procurement, invoicing, sourcing, supplier and contract modules; scope is broadening, not defensive.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: A live, technically-winning Formal Evaluation that just pulled two July group demos forward, but it is single-threaded through process-champion Joseph Pacheco with no economic buyer ever met and only a public-data ROI against a cost-ranked, undisclosed top-5 field - the one thing between us and control is opening the budget owner with a SABIC-specific value case.

win_position.text: Scores mid-band (about 55) not higher because, despite strong functional fit and re-accelerating engagement, no economic buyer has ever appeared and there is no SABIC-specific business case ahead of a cost-ranked top-5-to-top-2 down-select - Formal-Evaluation upside is capped until power and value are secured.

Functional fit is winning: Brian (Head of Legal US) said the CLM 'looks great' (17 Jun 2026), Jacco confirmed the SAP integration deep-dive 'gives a better understanding' (10 Jun 2026), and Zycus is the single-platform answer to SAP SRM end-of-life.

The decision has moved to an unnamed US/Europe leadership committee above Joseph Pacheco (Ivana Harris, 'waiting on leadership discussions', 19 Jun 2026) that Zycus has never met - a single-thread-to-power risk that could stall or lose the deal.

Cost is an explicit ranking axis yet leadership rejected the generic ROI and asked for a case study (26 Jun 2026); against an undisclosed top-5 field, competing on a public-data value model is a live loss risk.

deal_momentum.text: Buyer-initiated pull: Ivana Harris requested a bigger-group procurement demo (30 Jun 2026) and confirmed availability, with procurement (14 Jul) and supply-chain (15 Jul) demos now booked.
Evaluation broadened from procurement to IT/ERP to legal across four deep sessions (May-Jun 2026), and scope firmed from $250k to $518k on 11 Jun 2026.
A mid-June pause ('waiting on leadership discussions regarding evaluations', 19 Jun 2026) shows the committee, not the champion, now controls pace and no dated decision milestone exists.

customer_commitment.text: Buyer stood up a shared Showpad collaboration space and invited Pacheco and Jeroen Verhulst (29-30 Jun 2026), and asked for implementation cost and references - concrete evaluation investment.
Commitment is still process-level: the buyer owes commercial inputs for a tailored proposal (asked 10 Jun 2026) and has not named a decision date or the budget owner.

deal_risk.text: Economic buyer unmapped and unmet - the largest risk for a $518k P2P decision at Formal Evaluation.
Undisclosed top-5 rivals plus an informal no-RFP process with no strong compelling event beyond SAP SRM end-of-life leaves status-quo a genuine alternative.

economic_buyer_access.evidence: No EB named or met; decision sits with an unnamed US/Europe leadership committee (Ivana Harris, 19 Jun 2026).

business_case.evidence: Only a public-data ROI shared; leadership asked for a case study, no SABIC-specific numbers (26 Jun 2026).

engagement.evidence: Buyer-pulled group demos booked 14-15 Jul 2026 after a brief leadership pause.

customer_preference.evidence: Positive functional reactions (Brian: CLM 'looks great', 17 Jun; Jacco on integration, 10 Jun) and Zycus-specific next steps (more demos, references, case study), but no explicit preference over the undisclosed rivals and cost remains a ranking axis.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain is clear (SAP SRM end-of-life, invoice matching, scattered contracts) but no SABIC-specific numbers; only a public-data ROI and Nippon Gas case study shared - leadership asked for more (26 Jun 2026).

items.action: Ask the relevant stakeholder to name and broker a meeting with the Americas budget owner.
items.trigger: Decision moved to unnamed leadership; no EB ever met.
items.expected_effect: Opens the power gate that makes Best Case defensible and de-risks the down-select.

items.action: Run the 14-15 July group demos and win the wider procurement and supply-chain audience.
items.expected_effect: Deepens multi-thread and consolidates functional preference before ranking.

items.action: Build a SABIC-specific ROI from their invoice volumes and cycle times.
items.trigger: Leadership rejected the public-data ROI and asked for a case study.
items.expected_effect: Gives the buyer a defensible value case to rank on cost and justify $518k.

items.action: Ask the relevant stakeholder which vendors are in the top-5 shortlist.
items.expected_effect: Reveals the live field so differentiation and pricing can be tailored before down-select.

items.action: Co-author a written mutual evaluation timeline with a named down-select date.
items.trigger: No dated vendor-selection deadline; Nov close is 118 days out.
items.expected_effect: Converts an informal, no-compelling-event process into a dated path to close.

contributions.evidence: customer requested next meeting (from call evidence).

commentary.win_position: Win 38 is carried by momentum 65 vs stage-expected 52; preference strength +0.40 (weight 20). Offsetting it: competitive strength -1.00 (weight 15).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 65 (moving forward): seniority rising (from call evidence). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 0: no break-risk was observed in the swept evidence. Coverage is thin, so read this as 'nothing seen yet,' not 'nothing there.'
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 31 is anchored on the stage win (38), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 65, and dragged by risk 0. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 2 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (exec and champion, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.header: Sabic Innovative Plastics US LLC — Formal Evaluation · $518K · closes 1 Nov
cro_panel.intro: A live, technically-winning Formal Evaluation that just pulled two July group demos forward, but it is single-threaded through process-champion Joseph Pacheco with no economic buyer ever met and only a public-data ROI against a cost-ranked, undisclosed top-5 field - the one thing between us and control is opening the budget owner with a SABIC-specific value case.

blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.text: Scores mid-band (about 55) not higher because, despite strong functional fit and re-accelerating engagement, no economic buyer has ever appeared and there is no SABIC-specific business case ahead of a cost-ranked top-5-to-top-2 down-select - Formal-Evaluation upside is capped until power and value are secured.

Functional fit is winning: Brian (Head of Legal US) said the CLM 'looks great' (17 Jun 2026), Jacco confirmed the SAP integration deep-dive 'gives a better understanding' (10 Jun 2026), and Zycus is the single-platform answer to SAP SRM end-of-life.

The decision has moved to an unnamed US/Europe leadership committee above Joseph Pacheco (Ivana Harris, 'waiting on leadership discussions', 19 Jun 2026) that Zycus has never met - a single-thread-to-power risk that could stall or lose the deal.

Cost is an explicit ranking axis yet leadership rejected the generic ROI and asked for a case study (26 Jun 2026); against an undisclosed top-5 field, competing on a public-data value model is a live loss risk.

blocks.how: A deal at 'Formal Evaluation' anchors near 35. Win signals and momentum lift the read to 38.

bullets.text: Buyer-initiated pull: Ivana Harris requested a bigger-group procurement demo (30 Jun 2026) and confirmed availability, with procurement (14 Jul) and supply-chain (15 Jul) demos now booked.
Evaluation broadened from procurement to IT/ERP to legal across four deep sessions (May-Jun 2026), and scope firmed from $250k to $518k on 11 Jun 2026.
A mid-June pause ('waiting on leadership discussions regarding evaluations', 19 Jun 2026) shows the committee, not the champion, now controls pace and no dated decision milestone exists.

blocks.read: There's a live competitive threat plus execution risk — both below.

bullets.text: Economic buyer unmapped and unmet - the largest risk for a $518k P2P decision at Formal Evaluation.
Undisclosed top-5 rivals plus an informal no-RFP process with no strong compelling event beyond SAP SRM end-of-life leaves status-quo a genuine alternative.
